title: How the critic part mimics the voice of an abusive parent
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The voice that sounds like yours but isn't

You know the one. That voice in your head that says you're not trying hard enough. That you're lazy. That you're too much and not enough at the same time. It's relentless. It's cruel. And here's the thing: it sounds exactly like your own voice. But it isn't yours.

I spent years believing that voice was just me. My honest self-appraisal. My inner truth. I'd wake up and before my feet hit the floor, it would start. "You forgot to do that thing yesterday. You're falling behind. Everyone else has it figured out and you're still here, still broken, still failing." And I'd agree with it. Because it felt so familiar. So reasonable.

Then I started learning about Internal Family Systems, the model developed by Richard Schwartz. And I learned that the voice isn't me. It's a part of me. A protector part. A part that formed early, in response to real danger. A part that learned that if it could criticize me hard enough, maybe I'd finally do the right thing and avoid punishment. Avoid shame. Avoid being abandoned.

Stay with me here. This gets personal.

That critic part of yours? The one that sounds so logical, so certain, so harsh? It's not your . It's a fragment of your psyche that took on the job of keeping you safe. And it learned that job from someone. Probably from an actual person who criticized you, shamed you, or hurt you when you were small and defenseless.

I'm not saying your parent was a monster. I'm saying that the critic part often mimics the exact tone, the exact words, the exact rhythm of the person who first made you feel small. It's a recording. A loop. And you've been listening to it on repeat for decades.

Think about it. When you make a mistake, what does the critic say? Is it your voice, or is it your mother's? Your father's? Your grandmother's? The teacher who humiliated you in front of the class? The coach who said you'd never amount to anything? The voice might be inside your head now, but it didn't start there.

The science of internalized abuse

Bessel van der Kolk, the trauma researcher who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing that trauma isn't just a memory. It's a physiological state. Your body remembers what happened even when your mind tries to forget. And that includes the voice of the person who hurt you.

When a child grows up with a parent who is critical, dismissive, or outright abusive, the child's developing brain does something and tragic. It internalizes that voice. It makes it part of its own internal dialogue. Because here's the survival logic: if I can hear my parent's voice inside my own head, I can anticipate their reactions. I can pre-criticize myself before they do. Maybe I can avoid the hit. The yelling. The silent treatment. The withdrawal of love.

This is called introjection. It's a psychological term for taking in the voice of an external authority and making it your own inner critic. And it works. For a while. It keeps you on edge, but it also keeps you alive in a dangerous environment. The problem is, you carry that voice with you long after the danger is gone. Long after you've moved out. Long after the parent has changed or died. The critic part doesn't know time. It doesn't know that you're an adult now. It's still trying to protect the child you were.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why the critic felt so persistent. Because it wasn't just my own voice. It was the voice of someone who never learned to see me as a separate person with my own needs and feelings.

Janina Fisher, who wrote "Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors," calls these parts "survival strategies." The critic part isn't your enemy. It's a part of you that took on an impossible job. It tried to keep you safe by controlling your behavior through shame and fear. And it's exhausted. It's been working nonstop for years, maybe decades. But it doesn't know how to stop. It doesn't know any other way to keep you safe.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Fisher's work helped me see that the critic isn't me. It's a part of me that needs compassion, not more criticism.

The exact words your critic uses

Let's get specific. I want you to notice what happens in your body as I say these phrases. Don't analyze. Just notice.

"You're so stupid. Why would you do that?"
"You're never going to succeed. You don't have what it takes."
"Everyone else can handle this. What's wrong with you?"
"You're too sensitive. You're overreacting. You're making a big deal out of nothing."
"If you had just tried harder, this wouldn't have happened."

Do any of those feel familiar? Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach drop? Did you feel a rush of shame, or anger, or both?

Now ask yourself: who said those words to you first? Not the voice in your head. The real person. The one who stood in front of you and spoke them aloud. Because I guarantee you, the critic part didn't invent those phrases. It learned them. It memorized them. It rehearsed them until they became automatic.

Wild, right? The most intimate voice in your head might be a recording of someone who hurt you.

Pete Walker, who wrote "Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving," describes the critic as a "toxic inner voice" that mimics the abusive parent. He says the critic attacks you for things that were never your fault. It attacks you for having normal human needs. For being vulnerable. For being a child who needed care and didn't get it.

I remember the first time I realized my critic sounded exactly like my father. Not in tone or accent, but in structure. The same pattern. First, a judgment. Then, a comparison to someone better. Then, a prediction of failure. It was his script, playing in my head, and I'd been delivering his lines for thirty years without realizing I was reading from his playbook.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It helped me see that the critic wasn't just repeating words. It was repeating a pattern of emotional neglect. The message wasn't just "you're bad." It was "your feelings don't matter."

How to stop agreeing with the critic

Here's where it gets tricky. Because the critic part doesn't respond well to being attacked. If you fight it, it fights back harder. If you try to silence it, it gets louder. If you tell it to go away, it digs in. Sound familiar?

The approach that actually works is counterintuitive. You don't try to get rid of the critic. You get curious about it. You ask it questions. You treat it like a scared, exhausted child who's been doing a terrible job for a long time but doesn't know how to stop.

When the critic says "you're so lazy," instead of agreeing or arguing, try this: "I notice you're saying I'm lazy. I wonder what you're afraid would happen if I rested."

When the critic says "you're going to fail," try: "I see you're worried. What do you think would happen if I succeeded?"

Notice what just happened in your body. Did that feel weird? Did it feel fake? Did you feel a flicker of something else, maybe sadness, maybe relief? That's okay. The critic part has been running the show for so long that any change feels threatening. Even a gentle question can feel like an attack to a part that's used to being in control.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman wrote it, and she describes how recovery happens in stages. First, safety. Then, remembrance and mourning. Then, reconnection. You can't skip to the end. You have to start with safety. And safety for the critic part means not trying to kill it. It means listening.

I'm not saying it's easy. I'm saying it's possible. I've seen it in my own life. The critic part still shows up. It still has opinions. But now I know it's not me. It's a part of me that learned a harsh language in a harsh environment. And I can speak back to it with kindness instead of agreement.

The critic is not your enemy

I need you to hear this. The critic part is not your enemy. It's not evil. It's not trying to destroy you. It's trying to protect you using the only tools it has. Tools that were forged in a fire that should never have touched you.

The problem isn't that the critic exists. The problem is that you believe it. You treat its words as truth. You let it define your worth, your potential, your future. And that's not your fault. You were taught to believe it. You were trained to trust the voice that sounded like authority, like love, like safety. Even when it wasn't any of those things.

So here's what I want you to try. Just once. The next time the critic speaks, don't argue. Don't agree. Just say this: "I hear you. I know you're trying to protect me. But I'm not a child anymore. I can handle this."

Say it out loud if you can. Say it in your head if you can't. And notice what happens. The critic might get louder at first. That's normal. It's scared. But if you stay with it, if you stay curious, something shifts. The voice might soften. It might change its words. It might even go quiet for a moment.

That moment is the beginning of freedom. Not freedom from the critic. But freedom from being controlled by it. Freedom to choose who you listen to. Freedom to decide what voice gets to define your life.

You don't have to believe everything you think. Especially when that thought sounds like someone who hurt you.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
